key_points: The Mughals were descendants of two great lineages: Genghis Khan (mother's side) and Timur (father's side).
Babur was the first Mughal emperor (1526–1530) who captured Delhi after defeating Ibrahim Lodi at Panipat.
The Mughal Empire expanded greatly under Akbar, who became emperor at the young age of 13.
The Mughals did not follow the law of primogeniture (oldest son getting everything); instead, they often shared the inheritance among all sons.
Akbar's revenue minister, Todar Mal, carried out a careful survey of crop yields and prices for a 10-year period.
examples: Humayun: Faced a big challenge from Sher Shah Suri and had to flee to Iran for fifteen years.
Mansabdars: Officers in Mughal service who held a 'mansab' (rank) and were responsible for a specific number of cavalrymen.
Zabt: A revenue system based on the average prices and yields of crops over a ten-year period.
Sulh-i-kul: Akbar's idea of 'universal peace' which focused on honesty, justice, and peace among all religions.
formulas: Mughal Dynasty = Timurid Roots + Indian Conquests
Zat = Rank and Salary of a Mansabdar (Higher Zat = Higher Status)
exam_tips: Remember the sequence of the six 'Great Mughals': Babur, Humayun, Akbar, Jahangir, Shah Jahan, and Aurangzeb.
Note that the Mughals preferred to be called 'Timurids' rather than 'Mughals' (which reminded them of the Mongols).
Understand that 'Jagirs' were lands given to Mansabdars so they could collect revenue as their salary.
Identify that Abul Fazl wrote 'Akbarnama,' a famous three-volume history of Akbar's reign.
